concepts.label: Cryptocurrencies
concepts.reason: Central domain term indicated by the header; widely known but still a key topic anchor for the document.

paragraphs.text: Bitcoin was created in 2008 by an anonymous person or group using the pseudonym Satoshi Nakamoto. The whitepaper titled "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System" introduced a decentralized digital currency that operates without central banks or intermediaries.

concepts.label: Bitcoin whitepaper "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System"

concepts.reason: Named document title; verifying authorship and publication details benefits from grounding.
surfaceForms: whitepaper titled "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System"

concepts.reason: Specific historical attribution (date and creator) that is commonly cited but should be verified.

concepts.label: Blockchain technology
concepts.reason: Section header indicating the central topic; a domain-specific term but not a claim requiring verification.

paragraphs.text: A blockchain is a distributed ledger that records all transactions across a network of computers. Each block contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block, creating an immutable chain. Bitcoin's blockchain processes roughly 7 transactions per second, while Visa handles around 24,000.

concepts.label: Bitcoin blockchain throughput around 7 transactions per second
concepts.searchQuery: Bitcoin blockchain processes roughly 7 transactions per second source
concepts.reason: Quantitative throughput figure is verifiable and commonly debated depending on measurement assumptions; needs citation.
surfaceForms: Bitcoin's blockchain processes roughly 7 transactions per second

concepts.label: Visa throughput around 24,000 transactions per second
concepts.searchQuery: Visa handles around 24000 transactions per second source
concepts.reason: Specific performance number for Visa is verifiable and varies by definition (peak vs average); needs grounding.

concepts.label: Hash-linked blocks forming an immutable chain
concepts.searchQuery: each block contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block creating an immutable chain explanation
concepts.reason: Technical explanation that motivates immutability; benefits from authoritative grounding on how hash pointers enable tamper-evidence and what 'immutable' means in practice.
surfaceForms: cryptographic hash of the previous block

concepts.label: Blockchain as a distributed ledger
concepts.reason: Core definition of blockchain used as a foundational concept; widely established and less dependent on specific external verification.
surfaceForms: records all transactions across a network

paragraphs.text: The consensus mechanism Bitcoin uses is called Proof of Work, where miners compete to solve complex mathematical puzzles. The first miner to solve the puzzle adds the next block and receives newly minted bitcoin as a reward. This reward started at 50 BTC and halves approximately every four years.

concepts.label: Bitcoin block subsidy started at 50 BTC and halves about every four years
concepts.searchQuery: Bitcoin block reward started 50 BTC halving approximately every four years 210000 blocks
concepts.reason: Quantitative historical parameters (initial subsidy and halving schedule) are verifiable and commonly cited with exact block interval details.

concepts.label: Bitcoin Proof of Work consensus mechanism
concepts.searchQuery: Bitcoin consensus mechanism Proof of Work description
concepts.reason: Central technical characterization of how Bitcoin reaches consensus; worth verifying against authoritative sources.

concepts.label: Miners compete to solve cryptographic puzzles
concepts.searchQuery: Bitcoin mining compete solve mathematical puzzle proof of work cryptographic hash
concepts.reason: Describes the operational process of Bitcoin mining/PoW; benefits from grounding in accurate technical detail (hash puzzle).
surfaceForms: miners compete to solve complex mathematical puzzles

concepts.reason: Specific claim about who appends blocks and how rewards are issued; should be verified for correctness and nuance (coinbase transaction, fees).

concepts.label: Ethereum smart contracts
concepts.reason: Header indicates the section’s central topic: smart contracts in the context of Ethereum; useful as a stable domain concept, though not a claim requiring verification.

paragraphs.text: Ethereum was proposed by Vitalik Buterin in 2013 and launched in 2015. It introduced smart contracts—self-executing programs stored on the blockchain that automatically enforce agreements when conditions are met.

concepts.label: Ethereum proposed by Vitalik Buterin in 2013 and launched in 2015
concepts.searchQuery: Ethereum proposed by Vitalik Buterin 2013 launched 2015 sources
concepts.reason: Contains specific dates and attribution about Ethereum’s origin and launch that should be verified with reliable references.

concepts.label: Smart contracts as self-executing programs on the blockchain that enforce agreements when conditions are met
concepts.searchQuery: definition of smart contracts self-executing programs stored on the blockchain enforce agreements conditions met
concepts.reason: Provides an explicit definition/explanation of smart contracts that benefits from grounding to standard references (e.g., academic papers or official documentation).

paragraphs.text: In September 2022, Ethereum transitioned from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake in an event called "The Merge." This reduced Ethereum's energy consumption by approximately 99.95% according to the Ethereum Foundation.

concepts.label: Ethereum energy consumption reduced by ~99.95% after The Merge (Ethereum Foundation claim)
concepts.searchQuery: Ethereum energy consumption reduced 99.95% Ethereum Foundation The Merge
concepts.reason: Quantified reduction attributed to a specific source; requires grounding/confirmation and context for how the estimate was calculated.
surfaceForms: reduced Ethereum's energy consumption by approximately 99.95%

concepts.label: Ethereum 'The Merge' transition to Proof of Stake (September 2022)
concepts.searchQuery: September 2022 Ethereum transitioned from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake The Merge
concepts.reason: Specific dated event and technical transition that is central to the paragraph and should be verified with authoritative sources.
surfaceForms: Ethereum transitioned from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake

concepts.label: Ethereum Foundation
concepts.reason: Named organization serving as the attribution source for the energy reduction figure; useful entity for grounding context.

concepts.label: Decentralized Finance (DeFi)
concepts.reason: Header introduces the key domain term; generally well-known, so no verification is required, but it is central and canonical for the section topic.

paragraphs.text: DeFi applications allow users to lend, borrow, and trade without traditional financial intermediaries. The total value locked in DeFi protocols peaked at over $180 billion in late 2021 before declining significantly.

paragraphs.text: Uniswap, launched in 2018, pioneered the automated market maker model where liquidity providers deposit token pairs into pools. The protocol has processed over $1.8 trillion in trading volume since its launch.

concepts.label: Regulatory landscape
concepts.reason: Section header indicating the topic of regulations affecting the subject; not a specific factual claim that requires verification.

paragraphs.text: The SEC has classified most cryptocurrencies as securities, with notable exceptions for Bitcoin and Ethereum. The landmark SEC v. Ripple case in 2023 established that programmatic sales of tokens on exchanges do not necessarily constitute securities transactions.

concepts.label: SEC v. Ripple (2023) ruling on programmatic token sales not necessarily being securities transactions
concepts.searchQuery: SEC v. Ripple 2023 programmatic sales exchanges do not necessarily constitute securities transactions ruling
concepts.reason: Specific legal outcome tied to a named case and year; should be verified against the court decision and reporting on its holdings.
surfaceForms: programmatic sales of tokens on exchanges
do not necessarily constitute securities transactions

concepts.reason: Verifiable regulatory characterization attributed to the SEC; likely nuanced and requires grounding in SEC statements and enforcement history.
